summary: Southeastern Woodlands Native American people (self-designation Aniyvwiya/Tsalagi), historically centered in Georgia, North Carolina, and Tennessee. Sequoyah created an 86-character syllabary inspired by "talking leaves," enabling the newspaper the Cherokee Phoenix. Split into a Treaty Party (Major Ridge, Elias Boudinot) that signed the 1835 Treaty of New Echota and a National Party led by Principal Chief John Ross that opposed it; the treaty became the legal basis for the 1838 Trail of Tears, which forcibly removed the tribe to Indian Territory (Oklahoma). Stand Watie, the last Confederate general to surrender, was a member. Modern history includes Principal Chief Wilma Mankiller, the Cherokee Freedmen citizenship controversy, and current Principal Chief Chuck Hoskin Jr.

works.name: General / Giveaway Identifiers
works.description: How the Cherokee are identified in giveaway lines: a Native American/indigenous people who endured forced removal on the Trail of Tears, whose self-designations include Aniyvwiya, Tsalagi, and Anigiduwagi, and who today have federally recognized bands based in Oklahoma (Cherokee Nation, United Keetoowah Band) and North Carolina (Eastern Band).

examples: An eastern heritage center is found at Oconaluftee, the only remaining one in their original lands, and the heritage villages of Adams Corner and Diligwa are administered by a heritage center of these people in Tahlequah, which replaced their former capital of New Echota

works.name: Sequoyah's Syllabary
works.description: Sequoyah, inspired by the "talking leaves" (written documents) he observed while fighting at the Battle of Horseshoe Bend, single-handedly devised an 86-character syllabary for the Cherokee language. Its completion enabled rapid literacy across the tribe, religious translations, and the bilingual newspaper the Cherokee Phoenix.

clues.clue: Sequoyah created an 86-character syllabic writing system inspired by "talking leaves"
examples: One of these people created a syllabic writing system of eighty-six characters that was inspired by "talking leaves"
Sequoyah developed a syllabary for this tribe

clues.clue: A group of the Cherokee, including Sequoyah, fought at the Battle of Horseshoe Bend under Major Ridge
examples: A group of these people fought at the Battle of Horseshoe Bend under Major Ridge

clues.clue: Literacy among the Cherokee increased dramatically after Sequoyah designed the syllabary
examples: Literacy among this tribe increased after Sequoyah designed its name syllabary
These people began to create religious texts after the development of a writing system by Sequoyah

clues.clue: The syllabary enabled the bilingual newspaper the Cherokee Phoenix
examples: It published a newspaper called the Phoenix
whose bilingual newspaper, the Phoenix, used a script invented by Sequoyah

clues.clue: Robert Latham Owen, US Senator from Oklahoma, was the great-grandson of a Cherokee tribal member
examples: Oklahoma senator Robert Latham Owen was the great-grandson of a member of this Indian tribe. Sequoyah created a syllabary for the language spoken by this tribe

works.name: Treaty of New Echota & the Ridge-Ross Split
works.description: The Treaty Party, an unofficial faction including Major Ridge, John Ridge, and newspaper editor Elias Boudinot, signed the 1835 Treaty of New Echota without full tribal authority, ceding all Cherokee territory in the southeast in exchange for land in Indian Territory. Principal Chief John Ross, who led the opposing National Party, protested the treaty in a formal letter that was ignored by the US government; the treaty became the legal basis for the Trail of Tears.

clues.clue: In 1835 a faction of Cherokee not officially authorized by the tribe signed the Treaty of New Echota, agreeing to cede southeastern territory for land west of the Mississippi
examples: In 1835, the Cherokee signed this treaty, agreeing to give up their territory in the southeast and move to Indian territory. It was the legal basis for the subsequent trail of tears

A group of Cherokees claiming to speak for the entire tribe signed this 1835 treaty promising that the tribe would relocate west of the Mississippi. Chief John Ross's letter protesting this treaty was ignored

clues.clue: Principal Chief John Ross, the tribe's longest-serving chief, opposed the ceding of southeastern territory in the Treaty of New Echota and led the tribe after it forced relocation from Georgia
examples: These people's longest-serving Principal Chief, John Ross, opposed the ceding of their southeastern territories in the Treaty of New Echota
This group was led by John Ross after the Treaty of New Echota forced their relocation from Georgia

clues.clue: The Ridges (Major Ridge and John Ridge) and newspaper editor Elias Boudinot belonged to the tribe's unofficial Treaty Party, which feuded with John Ross
examples: The Ridges and Elias Boudinot were members of this tribe's unofficial Treaty Party, which feuded with John Ross
It was divided into a faction led by Major Ridge and one led by John Ross

clues.clue: Stand Watie was among those targeted for assassination for signing the Treaty of New Echota, but survived

works.name: Trail of Tears
works.description: The 1838 forced removal of the Cherokee from their southeastern homeland to Indian Territory (Oklahoma), carried out under the Treaty of New Echota. Marchers split into routes including the mostly aquatic Deas-Whitely Route (from Blythe Ferry) and an overland route ending at Webbers Plantation; some who died along the way are buried at McGinnis Cemetery in Illinois.

clues.clue: Undertook the 1838 Trail of Tears, forced west to Oklahoma
examples: identify this Native American tribe that undertook the 1838 Trail of Tears
name this tribe, forced to move to Oklahoma during the Trail of Tears

clues.clue: Route split into the mostly aquatic Deas-Whitely Route (from Blythe Ferry) and an overland route ending at Webbers Plantation; some who died en route are buried at McGinnis Cemetery, Illinois
examples: A number of these people are buried in McGinnis Cemetery, Illinois, which also names a cutoff associated with them. That route had one end at Webbers Plantation after splitting from the mostly aquatic Deas-Whitely Route at Blythe Ferry

clues.clue: Florence Owens Thompson, the "Migrant Mother" photographed by Dorothea Lange, was a member of the tribe
examples: Florence Owens Thompson, the Migrant Mother photographed by Dorothea Lange, was a member of this group

works.name: Dragging Canoe & the Chickamauga Resistance
works.description: During the Revolutionary War era, the Chickamauga faction of the Overhill Cherokee, led by Dragging Canoe, split off to continue resisting American settlers after other Cherokee leaders ceded land. They opposed the Watauga Association and raided the short-lived State of Franklin; in 1794 the US-organized Nickajack Expedition targeted the Chickamauga towns.

clues.clue: The Watauga Association was opposed by Dragging Canoe of the Chickamauga group; the Overhill group raided the State of Franklin

clues.clue: The 1794 Nickajack Expedition, led by white settlers, targeted the Chickamauga branch of the tribe, whose leaders included Dragging Canoe
examples: The Nickajack Expedition targeted the Chickamauga branch of this tribe, whose leaders included Dragging Canoe

clues.clue: Early leaders took the title "First Beloved Man"; while led by John Jolly, the tribe took care of teenage Sam Houston
examples: Early leaders of this tribe took the title "First Beloved Man." While led by John Jolly, this tribe took care of teenage Sam Houston

clues.clue: Settled in Tahlequah, Oklahoma after the Trail of Tears
examples: This people settled in Tahlequah, Oklahoma after the Trail of Tears

works.name: Nanyehi (Nancy Ward), the Beloved Woman
works.description: Nanyehi held the title "Beloved Woman," earned in part through war deeds, giving her a voice in tribal councils. During the Revolutionary War she warned colonial settlers of impending raids planned by her cousin, the Chickamauga leader Dragging Canoe, and she is credited with introducing dairy farming and loom weaving to the tribe.

clues.clue: Nanyehi, who held the title "Beloved Woman," warned colonists of impending raids from her cousin Dragging Canoe during the Revolutionary War
examples: During the Revolutionary War, a member of this tribe, Nanyehi, had a title meaning "beloved woman" and warned colonists of impending raids from her cousin Dragging Canoe

clues.clue: The tribe learnt dairy farming and loom weaving from Nanyehi
examples: These people learnt dairy farming and loom weaving from Nanyehi, who held the title "Beloved Woman"

works.name: Stand Watie & the Confederacy
works.description: Stand Watie, younger brother of the assassinated Elias Boudinot, led Cherokee Confederate troops during the Civil War, including a raid that captured the steamboat J.R. Williams and a wagon train at the Second Battle of Cabin Creek. He became the final Confederate general to surrender in the field.

examples: One leader from this group captured the steamboat JR Williams as well as a wagon train at Cabin Creek and later surrendered at Doaksville

works.name: Cherokee Mythology & Culture
works.description: Cherokee oral tradition includes a creation myth in which the water beetle dives to retrieve mud that becomes the earth, with mountains and valleys shaped by a buzzard's wings, and the water spider later brings fire from a sycamore after other animals fail. Selu, the corn spirit, creates corn from her body; other traditions describe the horned serpent Uktena, the giant hornet Ulagu, purification rituals, and the humorous "Booger Dance."

clues.clue: In the creation myth, the water beetle dives down and retrieves mud that forms the earth, shaped into mountains and valleys by a buzzard's beating wings
examples: These people believed the water beetle successfully retrieved earth, which formed mountains and valleys with the beating of the buzzard's wings
In these people's creation myth, the water beetle dives down to bring up mud, making the Earth

clues.clue: After owls failed, the water spider brought fire (from a sycamore, in one version) to the people, carrying it in a basket on her back
examples: After the owls failed, the water spider brought this people fire from a sycamore

clues.clue: Selu, a corn spirit, created corn from her body; in one version her stomach is rubbed, in another her body is dragged on the ground

works.name: Wilma Mankiller, Freedmen Controversy & the Modern Nation
works.description: Wilma Mankiller became the first woman elected Principal Chief of the Cherokee Nation and later received the Presidential Medal of Freedom. Membership in the tribe has been shaped by the Dawes Rolls, which separated members classified "by blood" from Cherokee Freedmen (descendants of people enslaved by tribal members like Stand Watie); Freedmen citizenship has been disputed and litigated for decades. The modern Cherokee Nation, currently led by Principal Chief Chuck Hoskin Jr., has also been central to disputes over blood-quantum identity claims and, in 2020, the McGirt v. Oklahoma decision confirming tribal jurisdiction over much of eastern Oklahoma.

clues.clue: Wilma Mankiller was the first woman elected Principal Chief of the Cherokee Nation and later won the Presidential Medal of Freedom (1998)
examples: Mankiller was the first female chief of this American Indian nation, whose bilingual newspaper, the Phoenix, used a script invented by Sequoyah
Wilma Mankiller, The first woman to be elected Principal Chief was awarded the Presidential Medal of Freedom in 1998

clues.clue: Mankiller was considered as a candidate to replace Andrew Jackson (who signed the Indian Removal Act) on the $20 bill
examples: Mankiller was a candidate to replace this president on the $20 bill. This president signed the Indian Removal Act, which forced the Cherokee to relocate to the Indian Territory

clues.clue: Membership in the tribe has been limited to descendants of those classified "Cherokee by Blood" on the Dawes Rolls (1898-1914), a census of the Five Civilized Tribes
examples: Mankiller limited membership in the Cherokee Nation to those who could claim an ancestor classified as "Cherokee by Blood" on this census of the Five Civilized Tribes living in the Indian Territory from 1898-1914
The names of those listed on the "By-Blood" section of the Dawes Rolls have complicated the classification of these peoples' "Freedmen," whose membership was opposed by Wilma Mankiller

clues.clue: Cherokee Freedmen, descendants of people enslaved by tribal members like Stand Watie, were long disenfranchised, requiring a CDIB card to vote under Ross Swimmer's executive order, before a 2017 court ruling granted their descendants tribal citizenship

In 2017, courts resolved a longstanding dispute about Black Freedmen who adopted this language by granting their descendants tribal citizenship in Tahlequah

examples: "Blood quantum" is controversially used as a requirement to claim this identity by groups like the United Keetoowah Band and Eastern Band, but Bill John Baker only claims one thirty-second heritage of this identity, despite having been a leader of this identity's Nation

clues.clue: Circe Sturm's book Blood Politics categorizes people with unsubstantiated identity claims as "outtalucks" and "should-bes"; Elizabeth Warren was a prominent example of a disputed claimant

The label "Black Dutch" was used to avoid discriminatory policies by people with this identity, many of whom were listed on the Baker Roll

clues.clue: Andy Payne, a Cherokee runner, won the "Bunion Derby" footrace from New York to Los Angeles along much of Route 66, which was unofficially named in 1952 for a Cherokee comedian (Will Rogers)
examples: A runner of this ethnicity named Andy Payne won the "Bunion Derby," a footrace from New York to LA. along much of Route 66. In 1952, the US Highway 66 Association unofficially named the road after a comedian of this ethnicity

comprehensive_summary: The Cherokee (Aniyvwiya, Tsalagi) are a Southeastern Woodlands people historically centered in Georgia, North Carolina, and Tennessee. During the Revolutionary War, the Chickamauga faction of the Overhill Cherokee, led by Dragging Canoe, split off to keep resisting American settlement, opposing the Watauga Association and raiding the State of Franklin, while Dragging Canoe's cousin Nanyehi, who held the title "Beloved Woman," warned colonists of his raids and is credited with introducing dairy farming and loom weaving; in 1794 the US-organized Nickajack Expedition targeted the Chickamauga towns. In the early 1800s, Sequoyah, inspired by the "talking leaves" he saw while fighting at the Battle of Horseshoe Bend under Major Ridge, single-handedly created an 86-character syllabary, which rapidly increased literacy, enabled Bible translations, and led to the bilingual newspaper the Cherokee Phoenix. As pressure to cede land intensified after the Dahlonega Gold Rush, the tribe split between an unofficial Treaty Party (Major Ridge, John Ridge, and editor Elias Boudinot) that signed the 1835 Treaty of New Echota, ceding all southeastern territory, and the National Party led by longtime Principal Chief John Ross, who protested the treaty in vain; missionary Samuel Worcester's arrest for residing on Cherokee land also prompted a landmark Supreme Court case. The Treaty of New Echota became the legal basis for the 1838 Trail of Tears, a forced march to Indian Territory (Oklahoma) that split into routes including the aquatic Deas-Whitely Route from Blythe Ferry and an overland route to Webbers Plantation, with some who died along the way buried at McGinnis Cemetery in Illinois; the "Migrant Mother" Florence Owens Thompson was a later descendant. During the Civil War, Stand Watie, Elias Boudinot's younger brother, led Confederate Cherokee troops, capturing the steamboat J.R. Williams at the Second Battle of Cabin Creek before becoming the last Confederate general to surrender in the field. Cherokee oral tradition includes a creation myth in which a water beetle retrieves mud to form the earth (shaped by a buzzard's wings) and a water spider later brings fire; the corn spirit Selu creates corn from her body, Kanati's sons release the animals from a cave, the horned serpent Uktena and the giant hornet Ulagu figure in hero stories, and the humorous "Booger Dance" satirizes outsiders. In the twentieth century, Wilma Mankiller became the first woman elected Principal Chief and later won the Presidential Medal of Freedom, while membership disputes centered on the Dawes Rolls' distinction between those "by blood" and Cherokee Freedmen (descendants of people enslaved by tribal members like Stand Watie), whose citizenship was restored by a 2017 court ruling. Current Principal Chief Chuck Hoskin Jr. has pressed tribal sovereignty following the 2020 McGirt v. Oklahoma decision, while "blood quantum" identity requirements and high-profile disputed claims (including Elizabeth Warren's) remain controversial, as documented in Circe Sturm's Blood Politics.
recursive_suggestions: Sequoyah / creation of the Cherokee alphabet — appeared as its own answerline (2015 George Oppen); rich standalone detail on the syllabary's creation, trial for suspected witchcraft, and Elias Boudinot's later edits
Indian Removal — appeared as its own answerline (2016 ACF Regionals) covering the broader 1830 policy across multiple tribes (Choctaw, Chickasaw, etc.), not just the Cherokee
Cherokee language — appeared as its own answerline (2021 The World As It Is); linguistic detail (polysynthetic structure, lack of labial consonants, modern revitalization efforts)
Worcester v. Georgia — the Supreme Court case is referenced but not detailed here; check for its own answerline
Wilma Mankiller — recurring figure with enough detail (first female Principal Chief, Medal of Freedom, Freedmen policy, $20 bill candidacy) to potentially support her own page
Trail of Tears — recurring as a discrete event across many tribes' removal (Choctaw, Chickasaw, Creek, Seminole); check for a broader standalone answerline
